stem: Which features suggest water diuresis (e.g., diabetes insipidus) rather than solute diuresis? (Select all that apply.)
choices: B. High urine glucose as primary driver
C. Hypernatremia risk if intake inadequate

stem: Desmopressin improves urine concentration in:
answer: B. Central DI (ADH deficiency)
rationale: Central DI responds to ADH replacement; nephrogenic does not.

stem: Nephrogenic DI can be caused by: (Select all that apply.)
choices: E. Chronic kidney disease affecting collecting duct responsiveness

stem: Arterial oxygen content (CaO₂) depends primarily on:
choices: B. Hemoglobin concentration and saturation (plus a small dissolved component)
answer: B. Hemoglobin concentration and saturation (plus a small dissolved component)
rationale: Most oxygen is carried bound to Hb; dissolved O₂ is small.

topic: Why PaO₂ Can Be “Fine” Yet DO₂ Low
stem: A patient can have normal PaO₂ but low oxygen delivery (DO₂) when: (Select all that apply.)
choices: D. CO poisoning (SpO₂ may appear normal) (concept)
E. Severe shock increases extraction and lowers SvO₂

stem: CO poisoning impairs oxygen delivery primarily by:
choices: B. Occupying hemoglobin binding sites and shifting O₂–Hb curve left (concept)
answer: B. Occupying hemoglobin binding sites and shifting O₂–Hb curve left (concept)
rationale: PaO₂ can be normal; Hb binding and release are impaired.

stem: Which findings can be present in CO poisoning? (Select all that apply.)
choices: B. Pulse oximetry that may read falsely normal (concept)

stem: A right-to-left shunt causes hypoxemia that responds poorly to high FiO₂ because:
choices: A. All alveoli are well ventilated
B. A portion of blood bypasses ventilated alveoli entirely
answer: B. A portion of blood bypasses ventilated alveoli entirely
rationale: Shunted blood never contacts alveolar gas; oxygen cannot “fix” bypassed blood.

topic: V/Q vs Shunt Response to O₂
stem: Which statements are correct? (Select all that apply.)
choices: A. V/Q mismatch generally improves with supplemental oxygen
B. True shunt improves minimally with oxygen alone
C. Hypoventilation (normal A–a) improves with oxygen but also needs ventilation correction
D. PE (dead space) is pure shunt physiology
E. Atelectasis can behave as shunt (V/Q ~0)

stem: HPV primarily serves to:
choices: A. Increase ventilation to hypoxic alveoli
B. Divert blood away from poorly ventilated alveoli to better ventilated regions (concept)
D. Decrease pulmonary artery pressure always
answer: B. Divert blood away from poorly ventilated alveoli to better ventilated regions (concept)
rationale: HPV improves V/Q matching locally but can raise PVR if diffuse.

stem: HPV becomes harmful mainly when hypoxia is: (Select all that apply.)
choices: A. Diffuse (entire lung) causing increased PVR and RV strain
E. Severe COPD with widespread hypoxia (concept)

stem: Pulmonary vascular resistance (PVR) increases most with:
answer: B. Hypoxia and hypercapnia/acidosis (concept)
rationale: Pulmonary vasoconstriction is triggered by hypoxia and worsened by hypercapnia/acidosis.

stem: Chronic lung disease can cause RV failure (cor pulmonale) via: (Select all that apply.)
choices: A. Chronic hypoxic vasoconstriction increasing PVR
B. Vascular remodeling in pulmonary circulation (concept)
E. Polycythemia increasing viscosity (can worsen PVR concept)

stem: A right shift of the O₂–Hb dissociation curve facilitates:
choices: A. Hb holding oxygen tighter (less release)
C. Increased oxygen binding at lungs always
answer: B. Oxygen unloading to tissues (concept)
rationale: Right shift decreases affinity → better unloading.

stem: Which cause a right shift? (Select all that apply.)
A left shift can be dangerous because it:
choices: B. Impairs tissue oxygen unloading despite adequate saturation (concept)
answer: B. Impairs tissue oxygen unloading despite adequate saturation (concept)
rationale: Higher affinity means less release to tissues.

stem: Tissue hypoxia can occur with normal SpO₂ in: (Select all that apply.)
In V/Q mismatch, oxygen often drops before CO₂ rises because:
choices: A. CO₂ diffusion is more efficient and ventilation can compensate until late
B. CO₂ is not produced in tissues
C. Oxygen is more soluble than CO₂
answer: A. CO₂ diffusion is more efficient and ventilation can compensate until late
rationale: CO₂ is more diffusible and ventilation increases to keep PaCO₂ near normal initially.

stem: Elevated lactate can reflect: (Select all that apply.)
A high SvO₂ in a shock patient can indicate:
choices: B. Impaired extraction/microcirculatory shunting (sepsis) or mitochondrial dysfunction (concept)
answer: B. Impaired extraction/microcirculatory shunting (sepsis) or mitochondrial dysfunction (concept)
rationale: High SvO₂ can be a warning sign in sepsis (delivery without utilization).

stem: Which statements are physiologically defensible without stereotypes? (Select all that apply.)
choices: A. Chronic hypoventilation can raise PaCO₂
B. Chronic hypoxemia can stimulate erythropoietin → secondary polycythemia (concept)
C. COPD always causes CO₂ retention early
D. V/Q mismatch is central to many chronic lung diseases
E. Chronic hypoxia can cause pulmonary hypertension

stem: CASE STUDY TAB Patient from house fire has headache, confusion, and “normal” SpO₂. PaO₂ is normal. LEFT (Mechanism options):
choices: A. CO poisoning reducing functional Hb and left-shifting O₂–Hb curve

stem: CASE STUDY TAB Patient with lobar consolidation remains hypoxemic despite high FiO₂; A–a gradient is high. Correct selections: LEFT: Physiologic shunt (V/Q ~0) from non-ventilated but perfused units MIDDLE: Recruit alveoli (PEEP) and treat underlying cause per protocol; monitor work of breathing RIGHT: Oxygen improves only as shunt fraction decreases with recruitment/resolution Rationale: True shunt responds poorly to oxygen alone.

CASE STUDY TAB Longstanding hypoxic lung disease patient develops peripheral edema, loud P2, elevated JVP, and exertional dyspnea. Correct selections: LEFT: Pulmonary hypertension increasing RV afterload → RV failure (cor pulmonale) MIDDLE: Address hypoxia and underlying lung disease, manage RV failure volume carefully per protocol RIGHT: Symptoms improve as oxygenation and RV afterload/volume status improve Rationale: Chronic hypoxia drives PVR elevation and RV strain.
Pulse oximetry cannot reliably detect:
choices: B. CO poisoning and methemoglobinemia (dyshemoglobins concept)
answer: B. CO poisoning and methemoglobinemia (dyshemoglobins concept)
rationale: SpO₂ can be misleading when Hb is abnormal.

stem: Methemoglobinemia can present with: (Select all that apply.)
choices: A. Cyanosis with relatively normal PaO₂
B. SpO₂ often around mid-80s and not improving with oxygen (concept)
E. Exposure to oxidizing agents (e.g., benzocaine, dapsone concept)

stem: Acute pulmonary embolism often initially causes:
choices: A. Hypercapnia as the universal early finding
B. Hypoxemia with respiratory alkalosis from hyperventilation (concept)
answer: B. Hypoxemia with respiratory alkalosis from hyperventilation (concept)
rationale: PE increases dead space → reflex hyperventilation and low PaCO₂ early.

stem: PE causes: (Select all that apply.)
choices: D. Improved oxygenation with no treatment
E. RV strain if large burden

stem: GnRH from the hypothalamus primarily stimulates the anterior pituitary to release:
answer: B. LH and FSH
rationale: GnRH drives LH/FSH release.

stem: Which pairings are correct? (Select all that apply.)
choices: A. FSH stimulates granulosa cells (follicle growth, aromatase activity concept)
B. LH stimulates theca cells to produce androgens (concept)
C. Granulosa cells convert androgens to estrogens via aromatase

stem: Mid-cycle LH surge occurs because:
choices: A. Estrogen exerts sustained negative feedback at all levels always
B. Sustained high estrogen switches to positive feedback, increasing GnRH/LH (concept)
C. Progesterone rises first and triggers LH surge
answer: B. Sustained high estrogen switches to positive feedback, increasing GnRH/LH (concept)
rationale: High estrogen (for sufficient duration) causes positive feedback → LH surge.

stem: The luteal phase is characterized by: (Select all that apply.)
choices: C. Basal body temperature rise (progesterone thermogenic effect concept)
D. Low progesterone and high FSH as hallmark

stem: Fertilization most commonly occurs in the:
answer: B. Ampulla of fallopian tube
rationale: Ampulla is typical site of fertilization.

stem: Implantation typically occurs about: (Select all that apply.)
choices: C. After the blastocyst reaches uterine cavity
E. During the window when endometrium is receptive under progesterone

stem: hCG is critical early in pregnancy primarily because it:
choices: B. Maintains corpus luteum progesterone production until placenta takes over (concept)
answer: B. Maintains corpus luteum progesterone production until placenta takes over (concept)
rationale: hCG rescues corpus luteum → progesterone supports pregnancy.

stem: Which are produced by the placenta? (Select all that apply.)
Placental estrogen synthesis depends on fetal contribution primarily because:
choices: B. Placenta lacks certain enzymes and relies on fetal adrenal DHEA-S precursors (concept)
C. Fetus provides LH to placenta
D. Maternal kidneys make estrogen directly
answer: B. Placenta lacks certain enzymes and relies on fetal adrenal DHEA-S precursors (concept)
rationale: Placenta uses fetal DHEA-S to make estrogens (feto-placental unit).

stem: Human placental lactogen (hPL) tends to: (Select all that apply.)
choices: B. Increase lipolysis to provide fuels for fetus
C. Lower maternal glucose availability for fetus always
D. Contribute to gestational diabetes risk
E. Mimic some growth hormone/prolactin-like effects (concept)

stem: Uterine blood flow in pregnancy increases primarily due to:
choices: B. Marked vasodilation and increased maternal cardiac output (concept)
answer: B. Marked vasodilation and increased maternal cardiac output (concept)
rationale: Pregnancy increases blood volume/CO and uterine vascular remodeling/vasodilation.

stem: Supine hypotensive syndrome occurs because: (Select all that apply.)
choices: A. Gravid uterus compresses IVC → decreased venous return
B. Decreased preload → decreased cardiac output
C. Left lateral positioning can improve symptoms
E. It is caused primarily by hyperkalemia

stem: Which physiologic change contributes to labor onset?
choices: A. Progesterone dominance strengthening uterine quiescence only
B. Increased oxytocin receptor expression and increased uterine prostaglandins (concept)
C. Decreased gap junctions in myometrium
D. Complete absence of estrogen effects
answer: B. Increased oxytocin receptor expression and increased uterine prostaglandins (concept)
rationale: Functional progesterone withdrawal and increased prostaglandins/oxytocin sensitivity promote contractions.

stem: Oxytocin causes uterine contraction primarily by: (Select all that apply.)
choices: A. Increasing intracellular Ca²⁺ in myometrial cells
E. Increasing gap junction coordination indirectly (concept)

stem: Milk production is primarily stimulated by:
answer: B. Prolactin
rationale: Prolactin drives milk synthesis; oxytocin drives milk ejection.

stem: After delivery, lactation is enabled because: (Select all that apply.)
choices: B. Prolactin remains elevated with suckling stimulation (concept)
D. High progesterone is required to initiate milk secretion

stem: An ectopic pregnancy can be dangerous primarily because:
choices: A. Implantation in tube cannot expand adequately → risk of rupture and hemorrhage
B. It always causes hypertension first
answer: A. Implantation in tube cannot expand adequately → risk of rupture and hemorrhage
rationale: Tubal rupture can cause life-threatening bleeding.

stem: Placental abruption can cause: (Select all that apply.)
choices: B. Fetal hypoxia from reduced placental perfusion
C. Risk of DIC due to tissue factor release (concept)

stem: CASE STUDY TAB Pregnant patient (third trimester) becomes dizzy and hypotensive when lying flat; symptoms improve when turned on left side. LEFT (Mechanism options):
choices: A. IVC compression → decreased venous return → decreased CO
B. Increased venous return from IVC compression

stem: CASE STUDY TAB Second-trimester patient develops rising glucose despite normal diet. Correct selections: LEFT: Placental hormones (hPL, cortisol, etc.) increase insulin resistance MIDDLE: Screen/manage glucose per protocol; nutrition + monitoring; treat if needed RIGHT: Glucose control improves maternal-fetal outcomes by reducing hyperglycemia exposure Rationale: Physiologic insulin resistance increases as pregnancy progresses.

CASE STUDY TAB Postpartum patient has adequate milk synthesis but infant isn’t getting milk due to poor let-down during stress. Correct selections: LEFT: Oxytocin-mediated milk ejection is impaired by stress (concept) MIDDLE: Support relaxation, breastfeeding assistance, latch techniques; encourage skin-to-skin RIGHT: Improved let-down and milk transfer as oxytocin release improves Rationale: Prolactin makes milk; oxytocin ejects it.
In menopause, which hormone pattern is expected?
choices: A. Low FSH and low LH
B. High FSH (loss of negative feedback)
answer: B. High FSH (loss of negative feedback)
rationale: Ovarian failure → low estrogen/progesterone → FSH rises.

stem: Physiologic features often associated with PCOS include: (Select all that apply.)
Testosterone is produced primarily by:
answer: B. Leydig cells (stimulated by LH)
rationale: LH → Leydig → testosterone; FSH → Sertoli supports spermatogenesis.

stem: Which support spermatogenesis? (Select all that apply.)
choices: A. FSH acting on Sertoli cells
B. Testosterone (from Leydig cells) acting locally
C. Sertoli production of inhibin (feedback concept)
D. Complete absence of intratesticular testosterone
E. Proper temperature regulation (cooler than core)

stem: The primary barrier preventing transepidermal water loss is the:
answer: B. Stratum corneum lipid matrix (concept)
rationale: Stratum corneum + lipids form the key barrier.

stem: Major burns cause: (Select all that apply.)
choices: A. Increased capillary permeability → fluid shifts (third spacing)
B. Loss of barrier → evaporative water loss
E. Risk of hypothermia due to heat loss

stem: Early “burn shock” is primarily due to:
choices: B. Plasma leakage from capillaries causing hypovolemia and decreased cardiac output
answer: B. Plasma leakage from capillaries causing hypovolemia and decreased cardiac output
rationale: Capillary leak + fluid loss → intravascular depletion.

stem: Which electrolyte patterns can occur after major burns? (Select all that apply.)
choices: A. Early hyperkalemia from cell injury
B. Later hypokalemia during diuresis/resuscitation phase (concept)
C. Hyponatremia due to fluid shifts/resuscitation (context dependent)
D. Hypernatremia as mandatory early hallmark

stem: The correct sequence of wound healing phases is:
answer: B. Hemostasis → inflammation → proliferation → remodeling (concept)
rationale: Hemostasis starts first (clot), then inflammation, then proliferation, then remodeling.

stem: The inflammatory phase includes: (Select all that apply.)
choices: B. Macrophage activity coordinating repair signals
C. Collagen cross-linking as primary feature
D. Cytokine release and debris clearance (concept)
E. Angiogenesis peak as the defining earliest feature

stem: The hallmark of proliferation phase is:
choices: B. Granulation tissue formation with angiogenesis and fibroblast collagen deposition
C. Collagen type I cross-linking predominance
answer: B. Granulation tissue formation with angiogenesis and fibroblast collagen deposition
rationale: Granulation tissue is proliferation.

stem: Remodeling phase involves: (Select all that apply.)
choices: A. Collagen type III replaced by type I (concept)
B. Increased tensile strength over time
C. Scar becomes stronger than original skin always

stem: Lymph flow is driven primarily by:
choices: A. A dedicated central pump like the heart
B. Skeletal muscle contraction, valves, and pressure gradients (concept)
C. Arterial blood pressure directly forcing lymph forward
answer: B. Skeletal muscle contraction, valves, and pressure gradients (concept)
rationale: Lymph relies on extrinsic pumps and one-way valves.

stem: Lymph formation increases with: (Select all that apply.)
Chronic lymphedema is characterized by:
choices: B. Protein-rich interstitial fluid with fibrosis over time (nonpitting later) (concept)
answer: B. Protein-rich interstitial fluid with fibrosis over time (nonpitting later) (concept)
rationale: Protein accumulation drives chronic inflammation/fibrosis; cellulitis risk rises.

stem: Lymphedema increases cellulitis risk because: (Select all that apply.)
choices: A. Protein-rich fluid supports inflammation and impaired immune trafficking (concept)
B. Reduced local clearance of bacteria
C. Skin barrier may be compromised

stem: The most direct cause of pressure injuries is:
choices: B. Prolonged tissue ischemia from sustained pressure exceeding capillary closing pressure (concept)
answer: B. Prolonged tissue ischemia from sustained pressure exceeding capillary closing pressure (concept)
rationale: Pressure collapses microvasculature → ischemia/necrosis.

stem: Shear contributes to tissue damage because it: (Select all that apply.)
choices: A. Distorts and tears microvessels in deeper tissues (concept)
B. Increases friction only at epidermis without deep effects
C. Reduces perfusion by angulating vessels
D. Is worsened with sliding down in bed

stem: Which local factor most directly impairs wound healing?
answer: B. Hypoxia at wound bed
rationale: Oxygen is critical for collagen synthesis and bacterial killing.

stem: Which systemic factors impair wound healing? (Select all that apply.)
choices: E. Adequate zinc and vitamin C intake

stem: Smoking impairs wound healing primarily by:
choices: A. Increasing oxygen delivery to tissues
B. Vasoconstriction and decreased oxygen delivery; CO reduces Hb oxygen transport (concept)
answer: B. Vasoconstriction and decreased oxygen delivery; CO reduces Hb oxygen transport (concept)
rationale: Nicotine vasoconstricts; CO reduces oxygen delivery.

stem: Major burns increase infection risk due to: (Select all that apply.)
choices: C. Increased bacterial translocation risk in severe stress (concept)
E. Invasive devices and wound surfaces

stem: CASE STUDY TAB Patient with large TBSA burn develops hypotension and rising lactate in first hours. LEFT (Mechanism options):
choices: A. Capillary leak → intravascular volume loss (burn shock)
B. Increased plasma oncotic pressure drawing fluid in
C. Primary cardiogenic shock from MI

stem: CASE STUDY TAB Patient post-lymph node dissection has chronic swollen arm that becomes firm over months and has recurrent cellulitis. Correct selections: LEFT: Protein-rich lymph accumulation → chronic inflammation/fibrosis and infection risk MIDDLE: Compression/lymphedema therapy per protocol; meticulous skin care and infection prevention education RIGHT: Reduced swelling episodes and fewer infections with consistent management Rationale: Lymph stasis impairs clearance and promotes inflammatory tissue changes.

CASE STUDY TAB Bedbound patient develops sacral skin breakdown. Staff note frequent sliding down in bed. Correct selections: LEFT: Pressure + shear → microvascular ischemia and deep tissue injury MIDDLE: Offloading/repositioning schedule, reduce shear (proper positioning), moisture control, nutrition optimization RIGHT: Improved tissue perfusion and reduced progression with pressure/shear mitigation Rationale: Shear distorts vessels; pressure blocks capillary flow.
A keloid differs from a hypertrophic scar primarily because it:
choices: A. Stays within original wound borders always
B. Extends beyond original wound borders (concept)
answer: B. Extends beyond original wound borders (concept)
rationale: Keloids extend beyond wound margins; hypertrophic scars stay within.

stem: Granulation tissue is characterized by: (Select all that apply.)
choices: D. Dense acellular collagen type I only
E. High oxygen demand and vulnerability to hypoperfusion

stem: Lymph nodes function primarily to:
choices: B. Filter lymph and provide sites for antigen presentation/lymphocyte activation (concept)
answer: A. Produce RBCs
rationale: Nodes filter lymph and coordinate adaptive immune responses.

stem: Chronic corticosteroids impair healing by: (Select all that apply.)
choices: A. Decreasing inflammatory cell function and cytokine signaling (concept)
B. Reducing fibroblast proliferation and collagen synthesis
C. Increasing wound tensile strength early

stem: Aqueous humor flows from the posterior chamber to the anterior chamber primarily through the:
answer: B. Pupil
rationale: Aqueous is produced by ciliary body → posterior chamber → through pupil → anterior chamber → trabecular meshwork → Schlemm.

stem: Increased intraocular pressure (IOP) can result from: (Select all that apply.)
choices: A. Impaired outflow through trabecular meshwork (open-angle concept)
C. Increased aqueous production always as primary mechanism
D. Blocked canal of Schlemm or trabecular dysfunction
E. Pupillary block precipitating acute angle closure (concept)

stem: A lesion of the optic chiasm classically causes:
answer: B. Bitemporal hemianopia
rationale: Crossing nasal retinal fibers (temporal fields) are affected at chiasm.

stem: Which statements are correct? (Select all that apply.)
choices: A. Temporal visual field projects to nasal retina
B. Nasal visual field projects to temporal retina
C. Nasal retinal fibers cross at optic chiasm (concept)
D. Temporal retinal fibers cross at optic chiasm
E. Post-chiasmal lesions produce homonymous visual field defects

stem: The afferent limb of the pupillary light reflex travels via:
answer: B. CN II
rationale: Light → retina → optic nerve (CN II) → pretectal area.

stem: The efferent limb of the pupillary light reflex includes: (Select all that apply.)
choices: D. Short ciliary nerves to sphincter pupillae (concept)
E. Sympathetic chain as the main constrictor pathway

stem: Accommodation for near vision occurs when:
choices: A. Ciliary muscle contracts → zonular tension increases → lens flattens
B. Ciliary muscle contracts → zonular tension decreases → lens becomes more convex
C. Ciliary muscle relaxes → lens thickens
D. Iris dilator contracts → lens thickens
answer: B. Ciliary muscle contracts → zonular tension decreases → lens becomes more convex
rationale: Ciliary contraction reduces zonular tension allowing lens rounding.

stem: Which statements are correct? (Select all that apply.)
choices: A. Myopia focuses images in front of retina
B. Hyperopia focuses images behind retina
C. Myopia is corrected with a divergent (concave) lens
D. Hyperopia is corrected with a convergent (convex) lens
E. Myopia is typically due to a too-short eyeball

stem: Light activation of photoreceptors leads to:
choices: C. No change in membrane potential
D. Immediate firing of action potentials in photoreceptors
answer: B. Hyperpolarization of photoreceptors (concept)
rationale: Light decreases cGMP → closes Na⁺ channels → hyperpolarizes photoreceptor.

stem: Which are true? (Select all that apply.)
choices: A. Rods are more sensitive in low light
B. Cones mediate color vision and high acuity
E. Rods are abundant in peripheral retina

stem: The primary structure converting mechanical sound vibrations into neural signals is the:
choices: B. Organ of Corti hair cells
answer: B. Organ of Corti hair cells
rationale: Hair cells in Organ of Corti transduce movement into receptor potentials.

stem: Which findings suggest conductive hearing loss? (Select all that apply.)
choices: A. Rinne test: bone conduction > air conduction (concept)
B. Weber lateralizes to the affected ear (concept)
C. Weber lateralizes to the unaffected ear
D. Rinne: air conduction > bone conduction always
E. Damage to hair cells is conductive loss

stem: In the vestibular system, angular acceleration is sensed primarily by:
choices: B. Semicircular canals via hair cell deflection in cupula
answer: B. Semicircular canals via hair cell deflection in cupula
rationale: Semicircular canals detect angular acceleration; otoliths detect linear acceleration/gravity.

stem: Which pairings are correct? (Select all that apply.)
choices: A. Utricle/saccule detect linear acceleration and gravity (concept)
B. Semicircular canals detect angular acceleration
C. Otolith organs use otoconia (calcium carbonate crystals) (concept)
D. Semicircular canals detect sound frequency

stem: Peripheral vestibular nystagmus typically has:
choices: B. A fast phase away from the lesioned side (classic teaching concept)
C. A fast phase toward the lesioned side always
answer: B. A fast phase away from the lesioned side (classic teaching concept)
rationale: Peripheral lesion reduces tonic firing on one side; brain interprets as rotation toward intact side → fast phase toward intact (away from lesion).

topic: Peripheral vs Central Vertigo Clues (Physiology Pattern)
stem: Features more consistent with peripheral vertigo include: (Select all that apply.)
choices: C. Nystagmus that fatigues and is inhibited by fixation (concept)
E. Hearing symptoms can accompany (tinnitus/hearing loss)

stem: A “blown” dilated pupil in uncal herniation occurs primarily due to compression of:
answer: B. CN III parasympathetic fibers (superficial) (concept)
rationale: CN III parasympathetics are superficial and compressed early → dilation.

stem: CN III compression can cause: (Select all that apply.)
choices: D. Inability to abduct the eye as primary deficit

stem: Loss of central color vision and acuity most directly implicates dysfunction of:
answer: B. Cones in fovea
rationale: Cones mediate color/high acuity; fovea is cone-rich.

stem: Which are physiologically plausible? (Select all that apply.)
choices: A. Cataract: lens opacity reduces visual clarity (scatter) (concept)
B. Glaucoma: optic nerve damage often associated with elevated IOP
C. Macular degeneration: central vision loss
D. Cataract primarily causes bitemporal hemianopia
E. Acute angle-closure glaucoma can cause halos and severe pain (concept)

stem: CASE STUDY TAB Patient has sudden severe eye pain, headache, halos around lights, nausea, and a mid-dilated poorly reactive pupil. LEFT (Mechanism options):
choices: A. Acute angle-closure → impaired aqueous outflow → rapid IOP rise
B. Open-angle glaucoma with slow painless course

stem: CASE STUDY TAB Weber lateralizes to the left; Rinne on the left shows bone conduction > air conduction. Correct selections: LEFT: Conductive hearing loss in the left ear MIDDLE: Evaluate external/middle ear causes (cerumen, otitis media, ossicles) per protocol RIGHT: Hearing improves when conductive issue resolved; sensorineural pathways remain intact Rationale: Conductive loss: Weber to affected ear; Rinne BC > AC.

CASE STUDY TAB Brief vertigo episodes triggered by head position changes; no neurologic deficits. Correct selections: LEFT: Otoconia displacement into semicircular canal causing inappropriate endolymph movement (concept) MIDDLE: Repositioning maneuvers per protocol; safety/fall precautions RIGHT: Vertigo decreases as otoconia reposition and abnormal canal stimulation resolves Rationale: BPPV is peripheral and positional.
BPPV episodes typically last:
choices: B. Seconds to minutes, triggered by position (concept)
D. Always constant dizziness without triggers
answer: B. Seconds to minutes, triggered by position (concept)
rationale: BPPV is brief and positional.

stem: Red flags suggesting central cause include: (Select all that apply.)
choices: C. Severe headache with neurologic signs
D. Nystagmus inhibited by visual fixation
E. Ataxia out of proportion to vertigo (concept)

stem: A patient with pulmonary fibrosis most directly has:
answer: B. Decreased lung compliance
rationale: Fibrosis stiffens lungs → ↓compliance (↑elastic recoil).

stem: Which findings are consistent with obstructive disease? (Select all that apply.)
choices: B. Air trapping with increased RV
D. Reduced TLC as the defining feature always

stem: Restrictive lung disease typically shows:
choices: A. Increased TLC and reduced FEV₁/FVC
B. Decreased TLC with normal/high FEV₁/FVC ratio
C. Increased RV with normal TLC
answer: B. Decreased TLC with normal/high FEV₁/FVC ratio
rationale: Both FEV₁ and FVC drop, but ratio is preserved or increased.

stem: Emphysema causes hypoxemia primarily through: (Select all that apply.)
choices: A. Loss of alveolar surface area
B. Capillary bed destruction increasing V/Q mismatch (high V/Q regions)

stem: A low DLCO most strongly suggests:
choices: A. Pure asthma with intact alveoli
B. Diffusion impairment or loss of alveolar-capillary surface area (concept)
answer: B. Diffusion impairment or loss of alveolar-capillary surface area (concept)
rationale: DLCO falls in emphysema/fibrosis; can be altered by anemia too.

stem: DLCO can be decreased by: (Select all that apply.)
choices: C. Anemia (less Hb to bind CO in test) (concept)

stem: In uncomplicated asthma, DLCO is often:
choices: B. Normal or increased (hyperinflation and capillary recruitment concept)
answer: B. Normal or increased (hyperinflation and capillary recruitment concept)
rationale: Asthma preserves alveolar-capillary membrane; DLCO can be normal/high.

stem: Surfactant: (Select all that apply.)
choices: C. Prevents alveolar collapse at low volumes (concept)
E. Reduces the pressure needed to inflate alveoli

stem: By Laplace’s law for alveoli, without surfactant, smaller alveoli would tend to:
choices: B. Collapse into larger alveoli due to higher pressure requirement
C. Have lower collapsing pressure than larger alveoli
answer: B. Collapse into larger alveoli due to higher pressure requirement
rationale: P = 2T/r; smaller r → higher pressure needed if T constant.

stem: Atelectasis can be caused by: (Select all that apply.)
Resorption atelectasis occurs when:
choices: A. Airway obstruction prevents ventilation, trapped gas is absorbed, alveolus collapses
C. Hyperventilation increases alveolar size permanently
D. Increased oxygen always prevents collapse
answer: A. Airway obstruction prevents ventilation, trapped gas is absorbed, alveolus collapses
rationale: Obstruction → gas absorption → collapse.

stem: High FiO₂ can promote resorption atelectasis because: (Select all that apply.)
choices: A. Nitrogen (which “splints” alveoli) is washed out (concept)
B. Oxygen is absorbed more rapidly into blood than nitrogen
C. It always increases surfactant production
D. It can worsen collapse behind partial obstruction
E. It guarantees improved oxygenation in all cases

stem: Central chemoreceptors primarily respond to:
choices: B. CSF pH changes driven by PaCO₂ (concept)
answer: B. CSF pH changes driven by PaCO₂ (concept)
rationale: CO₂ diffuses into CSF → H⁺ changes → drive.

stem: Peripheral chemoreceptors (carotid bodies) respond strongly to: (Select all that apply.)
choices: D. High PaO₂ as primary trigger
E. Hypotension severely reducing perfusion (can stimulate)

stem: Cheyne–Stokes respiration can occur in heart failure partly due to:
choices: A. Short circulation time causing stable feedback
B. Prolonged circulation time causing delayed feedback and oscillation in CO₂ control (concept)
C. Complete absence of central chemoreceptors
answer: B. Prolonged circulation time causing delayed feedback and oscillation in CO₂ control (concept)
rationale: Delay in feedback loop promotes periodic breathing oscillations.

stem: OSA contributes to hypertension via: (Select all that apply.)
choices: A. Repeated sympathetic surges from hypoxia/arousals (concept)
B. Intermittent hypoxia increasing oxidative stress/inflammation
C. Negative intrathoracic pressure swings affecting cardiac loading (concept)

stem: If physiologic dead space increases while total minute ventilation stays constant, alveolar ventilation (V̇A) will:
answer: B. Decrease
rationale: V̇A = (VT − VD) × RR; increasing VD lowers effective ventilation.

stem: Primary respiratory alkalosis can be caused by: (Select all that apply.)
choices: B. Early salicylate toxicity (hyperventilation phase concept)

stem: CASE STUDY TAB Patient has dyspnea and low DLCO. CT suggests emphysema; Hb is low-normal. LEFT (Mechanism options):
choices: A. Loss of alveolar-capillary surface area (emphysema) lowering diffusion capacity
B. Pure asthma with intact membrane
D. Increased Hb binding of CO

stem: CASE STUDY TAB Post-op patient on high FiO₂ develops worsening oxygenation with imaging suggesting dependent atelectasis. Correct selections: LEFT: Nitrogen washout + partial obstruction/resorption atelectasis worsened by high FiO₂ MIDDLE: Optimize ventilation, encourage lung expansion/recruitment per protocol; titrate FiO₂ to target RIGHT: Oxygenation improves as alveoli are recruited and collapse decreases Rationale: High FiO₂ can worsen resorption collapse; recruitment improves shunt/VQ.

CASE STUDY TAB PFT shows low FEV₁/FVC and increased RV. Correct selections: LEFT: Obstructive physiology with air trapping MIDDLE: Treat airflow limitation per protocol; monitor for hyperinflation/auto-PEEP risk RIGHT: Improved expiratory flow reduces trapping and work of breathing Rationale: Obstruction lowers FEV₁/FVC and increases RV.
Pulmonary edema reduces lung compliance primarily by:
choices: B. Increasing fluid in interstitium/alveoli, making lungs stiffer (concept)
C. Increasing elastic recoil in a way that always improves ventilation
answer: B. Increasing fluid in interstitium/alveoli, making lungs stiffer (concept)
rationale: Fluid-filled lungs are stiff; work of breathing rises.

stem: Cardiogenic pulmonary edema is promoted by: (Select all that apply.)
choices: A. Elevated pulmonary capillary hydrostatic pressure
B. LV failure increasing left atrial pressure
C. Increased capillary permeability as the dominant driver always
D. Can cause crackles and hypoxemia
E. Can cause orthopnea due to redistribution (concept)

stem: ARDS is characterized primarily by:
choices: A. Increased permeability edema with reduced compliance and refractory hypoxemia
B. Pure hypoventilation with normal A–a gradient
answer: A. Increased permeability edema with reduced compliance and refractory hypoxemia
rationale: ARDS is permeability edema + shunt-like physiology.

stem: Which findings support ARDS over cardiogenic edema? (Select all that apply.)
choices: B. Severe hypoxemia with high FiO₂ requirements
D. Clear response to diuresis always as defining feature

stem: Parietal cell acid secretion depends most directly on:
choices: A. Na⁺/K⁺ pump on luminal membrane
D. Carbonic anhydrase in RBCs only
answer: B. H⁺/K⁺-ATPase on luminal membrane (concept)
rationale: The proton pump secretes H⁺ into lumen in exchange for K⁺.

stem: Parietal cell acid secretion is stimulated by: (Select all that apply.)
Somatostatin reduces gastric acid secretion primarily by:
choices: B. Inhibiting gastrin release and directly inhibiting parietal cells (concept)
D. Increasing histamine release from ECL cells
answer: B. Inhibiting gastrin release and directly inhibiting parietal cells (concept)
rationale: Somatostatin is a key inhibitory regulator (D cells).

stem: Gastric prostaglandins help protect mucosa by: (Select all that apply.)
NSAIDs increase ulcer risk primarily by:
choices: B. Decreasing prostaglandin synthesis → reduced mucus/bicarb/blood flow (concept)
answer: B. Decreasing prostaglandin synthesis → reduced mucus/bicarb/blood flow (concept)
rationale: COX inhibition lowers protective prostaglandins.

stem: Pancreatic ductal bicarbonate secretion is stimulated primarily by: (Select all that apply.)
choices: D. Vagal stimulation can augment secretion (concept)
E. Somatostatin as a strong stimulator

stem: CCK primarily stimulates:
choices: B. Pancreatic acinar enzyme secretion and gallbladder contraction (concept)
answer: B. Pancreatic acinar enzyme secretion and gallbladder contraction (concept)
rationale: CCK is fat/protein signal → enzymes + bile delivery.

stem: CCK effects include: (Select all that apply.)
choices: B. Relaxation of sphincter of Oddi (concept)
C. Increased bile delivery to duodenum
E. Slowed gastric emptying (enterogastric effect concept)

stem: Fat in the ileum slows gastric emptying primarily via:
choices: B. Enterogastric reflex and hormones like peptide YY (concept)
answer: B. Enterogastric reflex and hormones like peptide YY (concept)
rationale: Distal gut signals slow proximal motility to optimize digestion/absorption.

stem: Motilin is associated with: (Select all that apply.)
choices: A. Migrating motor complex during fasting (concept)
B. Increased GI motility between meals
C. Secreted only after large fatty meals
D. Erythromycin acting as a motilin receptor agonist (concept)
E. Complete cessation of intestinal movement

stem: Most dietary long-chain triglycerides are absorbed as:
choices: A. Free fatty acids directly into portal blood as main route
answer: B. Chylomicrons into lacteals (lymphatics) (concept)
rationale: Long-chain fats → chylomicrons → lymph → systemic circulation.

stem: Which nutrients primarily enter portal circulation directly (not via chylomicrons)? (Select all that apply.)
Where is paracellular transport (leaky junctions) most prominent for solute/water absorption?
answer: B. Proximal small intestine (jejunum)
rationale: Jejunum has higher permeability for bulk absorption.

stem: Secretory diarrhea often involves: (Select all that apply.)
choices: B. Water following electrolytes into lumen
D. High stool osmotic gap as hallmark
E. Can be driven by toxins increasing cAMP/cGMP (concept)

stem: Cholera toxin causes diarrhea primarily by:
choices: B. Increasing cAMP → increased CFTR-mediated Cl⁻ secretion (concept)
answer: A. Blocking Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase
rationale: cAMP activation drives chloride secretion → massive water loss.

topic: Oral Rehydration Therapy (ORT) Why It Works
stem: ORT works in secretory diarrhea because: (Select all that apply.)
choices: A. SGLT1 sodium-glucose cotransport remains functional (concept)
C. Water follows Na⁺ absorption osmotically
D. It requires stopping all intestinal secretion first
E. It can reduce severity of dehydration even while diarrhea continues

stem: Bile acid diarrhea occurs when:
choices: A. Excess bile acids reach colon due to ileal malabsorption, stimulating secretion and motility
B. Bile acids are fully absorbed in stomach
D. Colon cannot absorb water ever
answer: A. Excess bile acids reach colon due to ileal malabsorption, stimulating secretion and motility
rationale: Ileal disease/resection → bile acids spill into colon → secretory diarrhea.

stem: Alkalosis can worsen ammonia entry into brain because: (Select all that apply.)

stem: The urea cycle occurs primarily in:
answer: B. Liver hepatocytes
rationale: Liver converts ammonia to urea for safe excretion.

Hyperosmolar chyme entering duodenum tends to:
